walkthrough.title: Choose Your Circuit
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Formula D. First, pick which circuit you want to race on. The game comes with a double-sided board. One side has the Monaco street circuit with tight corners and narrow lanes. The other side has a more open track. For your first game, either side works, but the open track is a bit more forgiving. Unfold the board and lay it flat in the center of the table so everyone can see the full circuit.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Cars and Dashboards
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a car miniature in their favorite color. Along with the car, you also take the matching dashboard in the same color. The dashboard is the board that tracks all your Wear Points during the race. You also grab the matching gear stick, which is a small dial or slider that shows what gear you are currently in. Finally, each player gets a scorecard to track damage throughout the race.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Dashboard
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to look at your dashboard. You will see six categories of Wear Points, or WP for short. These are: Tires, Brakes, Gearbox, Car Body, Engine, and Road Handling. Each category starts with a set number of points. These are basically your car's health. When you take damage during the race, you mark off WP in the relevant category. If any single category drops to zero, your car is eliminated from the race. Think of WP as your car's lifeline.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Six Dice
walkthrough.text: Formula D uses six different dice, one for each gear. This is what makes the game special. First gear uses a tiny die that rolls 1 or 2. Second gear rolls 2 to 4. Third gear rolls 4 to 8. Fourth gear rolls 7 to 12. Fifth gear rolls 11 to 20. And sixth gear is the big one, rolling 21 to 30. The higher the gear, the faster you go, but the harder it is to control your speed into corners. Set all six dice where everyone can see them.

walkthrough.title: Set the Gear Stick
walkthrough.text: Everyone sets their gear stick to first gear. You always start the race in first gear, no exceptions. During the race, you can shift up one gear per turn, or you can downshift, but downshifting costs Wear Points, which we will cover in the rules. For now, just make sure everyone's gear stick shows first gear.

walkthrough.title: Roll for Starting Positions
walkthrough.text: Now we determine the starting grid. Every player rolls the black twenty-sided die. The player who rolls the highest number gets pole position, which is the very front of the starting grid. Second highest gets the next spot, and so on. If two players tie, they roll again to break the tie between themselves. Place each car on the starting grid in order, with the pole position car at the front.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Track Layout
walkthrough.text: Before we race, take a look at the track. You will see straight sections with multiple lanes and corner sections marked with colored zones. Corners have a yellow box with a number in it. That number tells you how many times you must stop inside that corner zone during the race. Some corners say 1 stop, some say 2, and some say 3. These stops are the heart of the game. You will also see the start/finish line and the pit lane entrance.

walkthrough.title: Roll for Start Quality
walkthrough.text: Once everyone is on the grid, each player rolls the black die one more time to determine their start quality. This simulates how well your car launches off the line. If you roll a 1, that is a poor start. You stall and miss your entire first turn. If you roll 2 through 19, that is a normal start, and you proceed normally. If you roll a 20, that is a great start. You immediately move forward 4 spaces and can shift directly into second gear. Note your result and get ready to race.

walkthrough.title: Determine Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Turn order in Formula D is based on track position, not seating order. The car that is furthest ahead on the track always goes first. If two cars are in the same position, the car in the higher gear goes first. If they are in the same gear, the car on the inside of the track goes first. This means turn order can change every single round as cars jockey for position. Everyone set? Let's race.

walkthrough.title: Gear Selection and Rolling
walkthrough.text: On your turn, the first thing you do is choose your gear. You can stay in the same gear, shift up by one, or shift down. You can only shift up one gear at a time, so if you are in second, you can go to third but not fourth. Once you have set your gear, pick up the die for that gear and roll it. The number you roll is exactly how many spaces you move your car forward. No more, no less. This is the core of Formula D: choosing the right gear for the situation ahead.

walkthrough.title: Movement Basics
walkthrough.text: After rolling, move your car forward exactly the number of spaces shown on the die. You count every space along the track, following your lane. You cannot move through another car, so if a car is blocking your lane, you must change lanes to go around it. You can move into any open space, and your final position is wherever you land after counting all your spaces. Movement is always forward, never backward.

walkthrough.title: Corners and Required Stops
walkthrough.text: Corners are the most important part of the track. Each corner has a colored zone and a yellow box showing how many stops are required. A stop means your car must end its movement inside that corner zone. If the corner says 1 stop, you need to finish at least one turn inside the zone. If it says 2 stops, you need to end your movement inside it on two separate turns. If it says 3 stops, three separate turns. You do not have to stop on consecutive turns, but you must accumulate enough stops before leaving the corner zone.

walkthrough.title: Overshooting a Corner
walkthrough.text: Here is where things get dangerous. If you roll too high and your movement would carry you past the corner zone without making your required stop, you overshoot. For each space you overshoot by, you lose 1 Tire WP. So if you needed to stop in the zone but ended up 3 spaces past it, you lose 3 Tire WP. If you overshoot by more than 1 space beyond what your remaining Tire WP can cover, your car is eliminated. Also, if you lose your very last Tire WP from overshooting, your car is placed backward and you must restart in first gear. On a 2-stop corner, skipping both stops means elimination. On a 3-stop corner, making only 0 or 1 stop means elimination.

walkthrough.title: Straights and Lane Changes
walkthrough.text: On straight sections of the track, you can change lanes as part of your movement. You are allowed up to 2 lane changes per turn on a straight. Each lane change costs one space of your movement. You cannot return to the same lane you started in during that turn unless you are overtaking another car. Lane changes let you set up for corners, avoid blocked lanes, or position yourself for slipstreaming. In corners, you must stay in whatever lane you are in.

walkthrough.title: Gearing Down and WP Costs
walkthrough.text: Sometimes you need to slow down fast, and downshifting is how you do it. Shifting down 1 gear is free. But skipping gears when downshifting costs Wear Points. If you skip 1 gear, meaning you drop 2 gears in one turn, you lose 1 Gearbox WP. Skip 2 gears and you lose 1 Gearbox WP plus 1 Brake WP. Skip 3 gears and you lose 1 Gearbox WP, 1 Brake WP, and 1 Engine WP. You can never skip 4 or more gears in a single turn. That is simply not allowed.

walkthrough.title: Braking
walkthrough.text: If you cannot or do not want to move the full number of spaces you rolled, you can brake. For each space you choose not to advance, you lose 1 Brake WP. This lets you fine-tune your stopping position, which is crucial for corners. But if another car is blocking you and you physically cannot advance, the penalty is worse. For each space you cannot advance due to being blocked, you lose escalating WP. The first blocked space costs 1 Brake WP. Two blocked spaces costs 1 Brake and 1 Tire. It escalates from there, and being blocked by 7 or more spaces means elimination.

walkthrough.title: Collisions
walkthrough.text: When your car ends its movement right next to or directly behind another car, there is a chance of a collision. You roll the black twenty-sided die. If you roll a 1, you take a hit and lose 1 Car Body WP. Any other result means you are fine, no contact. This is a small risk, but it adds up over a long race, especially in tight packs. If your Car Body WP reaches zero, you are out of the race.

walkthrough.title: Motor Damage
walkthrough.text: High speeds come with engine risk. If any player rolls a 20 on the fifth gear die or a 30 on the sixth gear die, it triggers an engine check for everyone currently in fifth or sixth gear. All affected players roll the black die. Anyone who rolls 1 through 4 loses 1 Engine WP. This simulates the strain of pushing your engine to the limit. It is a shared risk, so one player's maximum roll can hurt everyone running at top speed.

walkthrough.title: Road Handling and Damage Markers
walkthrough.text: Some spaces on the track have damage markers on them. These represent debris, oil slicks, or rough patches. If your car passes over or lands on a damage marker space, you must roll the black die. A roll of 1 through 4 means you lose 1 Road Handling WP. A roll of 5 through 20 means you handle it fine. These hazards add up over 2 laps, so keep an eye on your Road Handling WP total.

walkthrough.title: Slipstreaming
walkthrough.text: Slipstreaming, also called drafting, lets you use the car ahead of you to gain extra speed. To slipstream, you must be directly behind another car, both of you must be in fourth gear or higher, and you must be going the same speed or faster. If all conditions are met, you add 3 extra spaces to your movement after rolling. You can even change lanes during your slipstream bonus spaces. If multiple cars are in a line, slipstreaming can chain, giving you 3 bonus spaces for each car ahead in the draft line.

walkthrough.title: Pit Stops, Elimination, and Winning
walkthrough.text: At the end of lap 1, you can pull into the pit lane to restore all your Tire WP. When you enter the pits, roll the black die. A roll of 1 through 10 is a quick stop: divide your roll by 2, round up, and move that many spaces forward in fourth gear. A roll of 11 through 20 is a slow stop: you stay in the pits until your next turn, then leave in fourth gear or lower. Now for elimination: if any of your six WP categories reaches zero, you are out. Finally, the winner is the first car to cross the finish line after completing 2 full laps around the circuit.

walkthrough.title: Meet Our Three Racers
walkthrough.text: Let's practice with a 3-player race. We have the Red car driven by you, the Blue car, and the Yellow car. After rolling for starting positions, Blue got pole position, you in Red are second, and Yellow is third. Everyone starts in first gear. The first corner is a 1-stop corner about 10 spaces ahead. Let's see how the first few turns play out.

walkthrough.title: Turn 1: Blue Shifts to Second
walkthrough.text: Blue is in the lead so Blue goes first. Blue shifts from first gear up to second gear and picks up the second gear die, which rolls 2 to 4. Blue rolls a 3 and moves forward 3 spaces. Good start. Blue is now 3 spaces ahead of the starting line, cruising along the straight.

walkthrough.title: Turn 1: Your First Move
walkthrough.text: Your turn. You are in first gear. Remember, you can shift up one gear per turn. Let's shift to second gear. Pick up the second gear die and roll. You get a 4, the maximum on that die. Move your red car forward 4 spaces. Nice roll. You are now 1 space ahead of Blue because you started 1 space behind but rolled 1 higher.

walkthrough.title: Turn 1: Yellow Moves Up
walkthrough.text: Yellow also shifts to second gear and rolls a 2. Yellow moves 2 spaces forward from the back of the grid. The pack is starting to spread out a little. After turn 1, the order on track is: you in Red (4 spaces from grid), Blue (3 spaces), and Yellow (2 spaces from the back). Since you are now in front, you will go first next turn.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Shifting to Third
walkthrough.text: Turn 2. You are in the lead so you go first. You are in second gear and the corner is still about 6 spaces ahead. Let's shift up to third gear. The third gear die rolls 4 to 8. You roll a 6 and move 6 spaces forward. You are now right at the edge of the corner zone. You did not enter it yet, but next turn you will need to think carefully about your gear choice.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Blue Shifts to Third Too
walkthrough.text: Blue also shifts to third gear and rolls a 5. Blue moves 5 spaces forward. Blue is now just behind you, also approaching the corner zone. The pack is bunching up as the corner gets close, which is exactly what happens in real racing.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Yellow Gets Aggressive
walkthrough.text: Yellow shifts to third gear and rolls an 8, the maximum. Yellow rockets forward 8 spaces and is now ahead of both you and Blue, right at the entrance to the corner zone. Yellow will go first next turn and will need to deal with the corner first. Sometimes being out front is not an advantage when a corner is coming.

walkthrough.title: Turn 3: Yellow Enters the Corner
walkthrough.text: Yellow is now in the lead and goes first. This is a 1-stop corner, so Yellow needs to end at least one turn inside the corner zone. Yellow is in third gear, which rolls 4 to 8. That might be too fast. Yellow could shift down to second gear, which rolls 2 to 4, ensuring a stop inside the zone. Yellow decides to stay in third and hope for a low roll. Yellow rolls a 7. That is too many spaces. Yellow blows through the corner zone entirely.

walkthrough.title: Yellow Overshoots the Corner
walkthrough.text: Yellow needed to stop in the corner but rolled too high. Yellow overshoots by 3 spaces past the end of the corner zone. That means Yellow loses 3 Tire Wear Points. Yellow marks off 3 Tire WP on the dashboard. Ouch. Yellow still has some Tire WP left but that was a costly mistake. If Yellow had lost all remaining Tire WP, the car would have been placed backward and forced to restart in first gear. This is why corner management is everything in Formula D.

walkthrough.title: Turn 3: You Approach the Corner Smart
walkthrough.text: Your turn. You are in third gear and right at the corner entrance. The corner zone is about 4 spaces long. Third gear rolls 4 to 8. If you roll a 4, you stop inside the zone, perfect. If you roll an 8, you overshoot by about 4 spaces and lose 4 Tire WP. That is too risky. Let's be smart and shift down to second gear, which rolls 2 to 4. That guarantees you land inside the corner zone.

walkthrough.title: You Stop in the Corner
walkthrough.text: You shift down to second gear and roll a 3. You move 3 spaces forward and land right in the middle of the corner zone. That counts as your 1 required stop. On your next turn, you can leave the corner freely. No Tire WP lost. Notice how shifting down to second gear before the corner saved you from the fate that hit Yellow. This is the fundamental skill of Formula D.

walkthrough.title: Turn 3: Blue Takes the Corner
walkthrough.text: Blue is right behind you and also shifts down to second gear. Blue rolls a 2 and stops just inside the corner zone. Blue also makes the required stop with no damage. Both you and Blue played the corner cleanly, while Yellow is ahead but 3 Tire WP poorer. Smart driving beats fast driving.

walkthrough.title: Turn 4: Exiting the Corner
walkthrough.text: New turn. Yellow is ahead so Yellow goes first, but Yellow already passed through the corner, so Yellow shifts up to fourth gear and continues along the straight. Now your turn. You made your required stop last turn, so you are free to leave the corner zone. Let's shift up to third gear and roll. You get a 6. You clear the corner zone and move onto the next straight. Blue does the same, shifting to third and rolling a 5 to exit the corner.

walkthrough.title: Turn 5: Accelerating on the Straight
walkthrough.text: Now you are on a straight and it is time to speed up. You shift from third gear to fourth gear. The fourth gear die rolls 7 to 12. You roll a 10 and fly 10 spaces forward. This is the thrill of Formula D. On the straights, you want to be in the highest gear you can manage. Blue shifts to fourth and rolls an 8. Yellow is already in fourth and rolls an 11.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Makes a Mistake: Skipping Too Many Gears
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Blue is in fifth gear approaching another corner and panics. Blue announces a shift from fifth gear all the way down to first gear. Hold on. That is skipping 4 gears, which is not allowed. You can only skip a maximum of 3 gears in one turn, dropping from fifth to second. Even dropping from fifth to second would cost 1 Gearbox WP, 1 Brake WP, and 1 Engine WP. Blue corrects the mistake and drops to second gear instead, paying those 3 WP.

walkthrough.title: The Braking Scenario
walkthrough.text: Blue is now in second gear and rolls a 4. But there is a car blocking the lane right where Blue would land. Blue cannot move through it and needs to stop 2 spaces short. Each space not advanced costs 1 Brake WP. Blue loses 2 Brake WP to stop behind the blocking car. Blue's Brake WP are getting dangerously low. Being stuck behind other cars is expensive.

walkthrough.title: Collision Check
walkthrough.text: Because Blue ended right behind another car, Blue has to check for a collision. Blue rolls the black die and gets a 7. No collision damage. Remember, only a roll of 1 means you lose 1 Car Body WP. Blue got lucky there, but the braking cost was still painful.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Forgets to Count Corner Stops
walkthrough.text: Later in the race, the cars reach a 2-stop corner. Blue speeds through the corner and only stops once inside the zone, forgetting that 2 stops are required. Wait, Blue needs to stop inside this zone on two separate turns. If Blue leaves without making both stops, that is a 2-stop corner violation. Blue only has 1 stop, so Blue either needs to pay Tire WP for overshooting, or if Blue skipped both stops entirely, Blue would be eliminated. Since Blue made 1 of 2 stops, Blue can pay the overshoot penalty in Tire WP and continue.

walkthrough.title: Slipstreaming in Action
walkthrough.text: On a long straight, you are directly behind Yellow. You are both in fourth gear. You roll your fourth gear die and get a 9. Because you are drafting behind Yellow, you qualify for slipstreaming: you add 3 bonus spaces to your movement. That gives you 12 total spaces. You rocket past Yellow and take the lead. Slipstreaming is one of the most exciting moments in Formula D.

walkthrough.title: Pit Stop Decision
walkthrough.text: As you cross the start/finish line at the end of lap 1, you have a decision. Your Tire WP are getting low after a few corner overshoots. You decide to pit. You pull into the pit lane and roll the black die. You get a 6. That is a quick stop. Divide by 2, round up: you move 3 spaces forward in fourth gear. You lost some track position but got all your Tire WP restored. You are ready for lap 2 with fresh tires.

walkthrough.title: Motor Damage Event
walkthrough.text: During lap 2, Yellow is in fifth gear and rolls a 20, the maximum. That triggers an engine check. All players currently in fifth or sixth gear must roll the black die. Yellow rolls a 3. That is between 1 and 4, so Yellow loses 1 Engine WP. You are in fourth gear so you are safe. This is the risk of running in high gears. Maximum rolls on the fifth and sixth gear dice can damage everyone at top speed.

walkthrough.title: The Final Stretch
walkthrough.text: It is the last few turns of the race. You are in the lead with Blue close behind and Yellow trailing after the engine damage. You shift up to fifth gear on the final straight and roll a 15. You fly across the finish line. You have completed 2 laps. You win the race. Blue crosses next, finishing second. Yellow, battered but alive, finishes third.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations, You Won the Race
walkthrough.text: You just completed your first Formula D race. You learned how to shift gears, navigate corners with the right number of stops, manage your Wear Points, use slipstreaming, and make a pit stop. The key lessons: slow down for corners, count your required stops carefully, and protect your Tire WP. They are your most precious resource. Ready to hit the track for real? Start a game and put the pedal down.

First corner (1-stop) is about 10 spaces ahead

Key lesson: slow for corners, protect Tire WP
Ready for a real race!

walkthrough.title: Tires Are Your Most Important Resource
walkthrough.text: Of all six Wear Point categories, Tires are the ones you will burn through fastest. Every overshoot costs Tire WP, and overshooting corners is the most common thing that happens in a game of Formula D. Protect your tires above all else. When in doubt, shift down one extra gear before a corner. Losing a turn of speed is always better than losing 3 or 4 Tire WP in one overshoot.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Rush Into High Gears Before Corners
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to shift into fifth or sixth gear and blast down a straight, but always check how far the next corner is. If a corner is coming up in 10 to 15 spaces, you may not have time to safely gear down. Remember, you can only shift down one gear per turn for free, and skipping gears costs WP. Plan your gearing 2 to 3 turns ahead of each corner.

walkthrough.title: Count Spaces to the Corner Entry
walkthrough.text: Before you choose your gear, count the spaces between your car and the start of the next corner zone. Then look at the die ranges. If you are 5 spaces from the corner and in third gear, which rolls 4 to 8, the best case is you land right at the entrance and the worst case is you overshoot by 3. That is too risky. Drop to second gear, which rolls 2 to 4, and position yourself perfectly.

walkthrough.title: Braking Is Expensive
walkthrough.text: Unlike real racing, braking in Formula D costs you Wear Points. Every time you choose not to advance a space you rolled, it costs 1 Brake WP. So if you roll a 10 in fourth gear but only want to move 7 spaces, that costs 3 Brake WP. That is a huge price. It is almost always better to be in the right gear than to rely on burning Brake WP to compensate for being too fast.

walkthrough.title: Slipstreaming Is Powerful
walkthrough.text: Three free spaces of movement is enormous. On a long straight, try to position yourself directly behind another car heading into the straight. When you slipstream, those 3 bonus spaces come after your die roll, so you can use them to pull ahead. If you can chain slipstreams through multiple cars, you can gain 6, 9, or even more bonus spaces in a single turn.

walkthrough.title: Pit Stop Timing Matters
walkthrough.text: You can only pit at the end of lap 1. If your Tire WP are comfortable, you might skip the pit and save time. But if you are low on tires, pitting is essential because without tires you cannot survive the corners of lap 2. The gamble is whether you get a quick stop or a slow stop. A quick stop barely costs any time. A slow stop means you sit for an entire turn. Weigh your Tire WP against track position before deciding.

walkthrough.title: Watch Your Dashboard Constantly
walkthrough.text: It is easy to forget about one WP category while focusing on others. Keep an eye on all six categories throughout the race. Engine WP can sneak down to dangerous levels if there are multiple motor damage checks. Road Handling gets chipped away by damage markers. Gearbox WP drain if you downshift aggressively. Any single category hitting zero means instant elimination, no matter how well the rest of your car is doing.

walkthrough.title: Position Beats Speed
walkthrough.text: In Formula D, where you are on the track is more important than how fast you are going at any given moment. Being in the right lane before a corner, having the inside line, or being positioned for a slipstream on the next straight are all more valuable than rolling one extra space. Think like a real racer: set up your car for the next section of track, not just this turn.

summary.title: Tires Are Your Most Important Resource
bullets: Overshooting corners is the #1 way to lose Tire WP
When in doubt, shift down an extra gear before corners

summary.title: Do Not Rush Into High Gears Before Corners
bullets: Plan gearing 2-3 turns ahead of each corner
Downshifting costs WP; plan ahead to avoid emergency drops

summary.title: Count Spaces to the Corner Entry
bullets: Count spaces to the corner zone before choosing gear
Compare distance to your die's range to assess risk

summary.title: Braking Is Expensive
bullets: -1 Brake WP per space not advanced
Better to be in the right gear than to rely on braking

summary.title: Slipstreaming Is Powerful
bullets: +3 free spaces per car drafted behind
Position behind other cars on straights for big gains

summary.title: Pit Stop Timing Matters
bullets: Only available at end of lap 1
Quick stop barely costs time; slow stop costs a full turn
Low tires = must pit; healthy tires = consider skipping

summary.title: Watch Your Dashboard Constantly
bullets: Any single WP category at 0 = eliminated
Engine, Road Handling, Gearbox can sneak down unexpectedly

summary.title: Position Beats Speed
bullets: Right lane and inside line > raw speed
Set up for the next track section, not just this turn

walkthrough.title: Optimal Gear Shifting Through Corners
walkthrough.text: The best drivers in Formula D know exactly which gear to be in for each corner. The key is matching your die range to the corner zone length. If a corner zone is 3 spaces long and requires 1 stop, second gear, which rolls 2 to 4, is perfect. You are guaranteed to land inside the zone. If the zone is 5 spaces long, third gear, which rolls 4 to 8, might work but has risk on the high end. Master the die ranges and you will master the corners.

walkthrough.title: Tire Conservation vs Speed Tradeoff
walkthrough.text: Aggressive drivers burn through Tire WP early but build a lead. Conservative drivers save tires but trail the pack. The sweet spot depends on the circuit. On a track with many tight corners, conserve tires because you will need every last one. On an open track with long straights and gentle corners, you can afford to push harder and accept an occasional overshoot. Know the track before you decide your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Track
walkthrough.text: Before the race starts, study the entire circuit. Identify the danger corners: those with 2 or 3 required stops, short zones, or zones that come right after long straights where you will be in high gear. Plan your entire gearing strategy for the lap. Which corners will you take in second gear? Which can you push in third? Where will you accelerate to fifth or sixth? Having a plan before you start gives you a massive advantage over players who react turn by turn.

walkthrough.title: Slipstream Chains and Positioning
walkthrough.text: Advanced players set up slipstream chains deliberately. If you can position yourself behind a line of 2 or 3 cars on a straight, you can gain 6 or 9 bonus spaces in a single turn. This means sometimes it is better to hold back slightly and draft than to be out front alone. On tracks with long straights, the car in first place is actually at a disadvantage because everyone behind can slipstream past.

walkthrough.title: Blocking Positions
walkthrough.text: Defensive driving matters. If you are in the lead approaching a corner, try to position your car so that opponents behind you have to change lanes or brake to get around. On narrow track sections, a well-placed car can force opponents to waste Brake WP or take suboptimal lines. This is especially effective in corners where lane changes are not allowed.

walkthrough.title: When to Sacrifice WP for Position
walkthrough.text: Sometimes burning a few WP is worth it. If you can gain 3 positions by staying in a higher gear and accepting an overshoot of 1 or 2 Tire WP, that might be a good trade. The calculus changes as the race progresses. Early in the race, WP are precious because you have a full lap ahead. Late in the race, especially near the finish, you can afford to burn whatever you have left because you just need to cross the line.

walkthrough.title: Pit Stop Strategy
walkthrough.text: The pit stop decision is critical. If you are in the lead with healthy tires, skipping the pit gives you a massive advantage while others lose time. But if you skip and your tires run out mid-corner on lap 2, you are done. Watch what other players do too. If the car behind you pits, you gain free distance. If neither of you pit, the race stays close. If you pit and they do not, you need to make up time. Calculate your remaining Tire WP against the number of corners in lap 2.

walkthrough.title: Construct-Your-Car WP Distribution
walkthrough.text: In the optional construct-your-car variant, you distribute 20 WP across all six categories. The best distribution depends on the track. For a corner-heavy track, load up on Tires, perhaps 6 or 7, and keep Brakes at 3 or 4. For an open track, you can reduce Tires to 4 and boost Engine and Road Handling. Gearbox is often safe at 1 or 2 if you plan your shifting well. Car Body can stay low at 1 or 2 since collisions are infrequent.

walkthrough.title: The Risk Calculation
walkthrough.text: Every gear choice in Formula D is a probability calculation. Third gear has a 1 in 5 chance of rolling its maximum. Fourth gear has a 1 in 6 chance. Knowing these odds lets you make informed decisions. If you need to roll 5 or less on a third gear die to stop in a corner zone, you have a 2 in 5 chance of overshooting, which is a 40 percent risk. Is that acceptable? That depends on your Tire WP and race position. Get comfortable thinking in these terms.

walkthrough.title: Adapting Mid-Race
walkthrough.text: No plan survives contact with the dice. The best Formula D players adapt their strategy on every turn. If you lose more Tire WP than expected in an early corner, switch to a conservative approach for the next few corners. If you get lucky with low rolls through a tough section, capitalize by pushing harder on the next straight. Watch other players' dashboards too. If the car ahead is low on Brakes, they might have to let you by rather than block. Racing is as much about reading your opponents as reading the track.

summary.title: Optimal Gear Shifting Through Corners
bullets: Match die range to corner zone length
2nd gear (2-4) for short zones, 3rd (4-8) for longer zones
Goal: guarantee landing inside the zone

summary.title: Tire Conservation vs Speed Tradeoff
bullets: Corner-heavy tracks: conserve tires
Open tracks: can push harder, accept occasional overshoots
Know the circuit before choosing your strategy

summary.title: Reading the Track
bullets: Study the full circuit before the race starts
Identify danger corners (2-3 stops, short zones)
Pre-plan your gear for every section of the lap

summary.title: Slipstream Chains and Positioning
bullets: Draft behind 2-3 cars for 6-9 bonus spaces
Being in 1st on a long straight = disadvantage (no draft)
Sometimes hold back to set up a slipstream pass

summary.title: Blocking Positions
bullets: Position car to force opponents into lane changes or braking
Most effective in corners where lane changes are banned
Defensive driving can drain opponents' Brake/Tire WP

summary.title: When to Sacrifice WP for Position
bullets: Early race: WP are precious, be conservative
Late race: burn remaining WP to cross the finish
Gaining 3 positions may be worth 1-2 Tire WP

summary.title: Pit Stop Strategy
bullets: Healthy tires + leading = consider skipping pit
Calculate remaining Tire WP vs corners in lap 2
Watch opponents' pit decisions to gauge position

summary.title: Construct-Your-Car WP Distribution
bullets: 20 WP to distribute across 6 categories
Corner-heavy track: Tires 6-7, Brakes 3-4
Open track: Tires 4, boost Engine and Road Handling
Gearbox and Car Body can stay at 1-2

summary.title: The Risk Calculation
bullets: Know the odds: 3rd gear has 1-in-5 chance of max roll
Calculate overshoot probability before choosing gear
Acceptable risk depends on current Tire WP and position

summary.title: Adapting Mid-Race
bullets: Adjust strategy based on actual WP losses vs plan
Watch opponents' dashboards for weaknesses
Lucky rolls = push harder; unlucky = go conservative

entries.definition: The smallest die in the game, rolling either 1 or 2. Used at the start and in tight corners where you need maximum control. You always begin the race in 1st gear.
Rolls 2 to 4. The safest gear for approaching and navigating corners. Provides predictable, short movement with low risk of overshooting.
Rolls 4 to 8. A versatile mid-range gear useful for longer corner zones or transitioning between corners and straights. Has moderate overshoot risk.
Rolls 7 to 12. The threshold between cruising and racing speed. Required for slipstreaming. Good for straights but dangerous near corners.
Rolls 11 to 20. High-speed gear for long straights. Rolling a 20 triggers a motor damage check for all cars in 5th or 6th gear.
Rolls 21 to 30. Maximum speed. Rolling a 30 triggers a motor damage check for all cars in 5th or 6th gear. Use only on the longest straights.
Moving to the next higher gear at the start of your turn. You can only shift up by one gear per turn. Shifting up is always free and has no WP cost.

Dropping to a lower gear at the start of your turn. Dropping 1 gear is free. Skipping gears costs WP: skip 1 = -1 Gearbox; skip 2 = -1 Gearbox/-1 Brake; skip 3 = -1 Gearbox/-1 Brake/-1 Engine. Cannot skip 4+.

The most frequently used WP category. Lost when overshooting corners (1 per space overshoot) and in some braking situations. Restored during pit stops. Losing the last Tire WP places the car backward in 1st gear.
Lost when voluntarily braking (1 per space not advanced), when forced to stop due to blocking (escalating cost), and when skipping 2+ gears while downshifting. Reaching 0 = eliminated.
Lost when skipping gears during downshifting. Any gear skip (dropping more than 1 gear) costs 1 Gearbox WP. Reaching 0 = eliminated.
Lost from collisions. When you end your move next to or behind another car, roll the black die. A roll of 1 = lose 1 Car Body WP. Reaching 0 = eliminated.
Lost from motor damage checks (triggered when someone rolls max in 5th or 6th gear) and from skipping 3 gears while downshifting. Roll 1-4 on black die = lose 1 Engine WP. Reaching 0 = eliminated.
Lost when passing over or landing on damage marker spaces on the track. Roll the black die: 1-4 = lose 1 Road Handling WP. Reaching 0 = eliminated.
If any single Wear Point category reaches zero, the car is immediately eliminated from the race. This applies to all six categories: Tires, Brakes, Gearbox, Car Body, Engine, and Road Handling.
A colored section of the track representing a curve. Each corner zone has a yellow box with a number indicating how many times a car must stop (end its movement) inside the zone before leaving.
The number of times a car must end its turn inside a corner zone. 1-stop corners require 1 stop, 2-stop corners require 2, and 3-stop corners require 3. Each stop must be on a separate turn.

When a car's movement carries it past a corner zone without making a required stop. Costs 1 Tire WP per space overshot. Overshooting beyond remaining Tire WP = eliminated. Losing last Tire WP = placed backward, restart in 1st gear.
On a corner requiring 2 stops, a car that makes 0 stops is eliminated. A car that makes 1 stop pays the overshoot Tire WP penalty for the missed stop.
On a corner requiring 3 stops, a car that makes 0 or only 1 stop is eliminated. A car that makes 2 stops pays the overshoot penalty for the missed stop.

Moving to an adjacent lane during movement on a straight. Maximum 2 lane changes per turn on straights. Each lane change costs 1 space of movement. Cannot return to the same lane unless overtaking another car.
During corner sections, cars must stay in their current lane. Lane changes are not permitted within a corner zone.
Passing another car by changing lanes to go around them. The exception to the same-lane-return rule: you may return to your original lane if you pass a car in the process.
The leader goes first each round. If two cars share a position, the car in higher gear goes first. If same gear, the car on the inside of the track goes first. Turn order updates every round.
Choosing not to advance the full number of spaces rolled. Costs 1 Brake WP per space not advanced. Used to fine-tune stopping position, especially for corners.
When a car cannot advance because other cars block the way. Escalating WP cost: 1 space blocked = -1 Brake; 2 spaces = -1 Brake/-1 Tire; costs increase per space up to 7 spaces blocked = eliminated.
Occurs when a car ends its move next to or directly behind another car. Roll the black d20: a result of 1 = lose 1 Car Body WP. Result of 2-20 = no damage.
Triggered when any player rolls 20 on the 5th gear die or 30 on the 6th gear die. All players currently in 5th or 6th gear roll the black die. A result of 1-4 = lose 1 Engine WP.
Spaces on the track marked with hazard icons representing debris, oil, or rough road. Cars passing over or landing on these spaces roll the black die: 1-4 = lose 1 Road Handling WP; 5-20 = safe.

Escalating costs when blocked: 1 space = -1 Brake WP; 2 spaces = -1 Brake/-1 Tire WP; 3 spaces = -1 Brake/-1 Tire/-1 Gearbox WP. Continues escalating up to 7 spaces blocked = eliminated from the race.

When directly behind another car, both in 4th gear or higher, and moving at the same speed or faster: add 3 bonus spaces to your movement. Can chain through multiple cars. Can lane change during bonus movement.
When multiple cars are lined up, the trailing car gains 3 bonus spaces for each car in the draft line ahead of it. Example: behind 3 cars in a line = +9 bonus spaces.

Available at the end of lap 1 by entering the pit lane. Restores all Tire WP. Roll black die: 1-10 = quick stop (move roll/2 rounded up spaces in 4th gear); 11-20 = slow stop (stay until next turn, leave in 4th or lower).
Black die roll of 1-10. Divide the roll by 2 (round up) and move that many spaces forward in 4th gear. Minimal time lost; you rejoin the race immediately.
Black die roll of 11-20. The car stays in the pits until the next round. On the following turn, the car leaves the pit lane in 4th gear or lower. Costs a full turn of position.
Each player's personal board that tracks all six Wear Point categories. Players mark off WP throughout the race. If any category reaches zero, that car is eliminated.
A dial or slider each player uses to indicate their current gear (1st through 6th). Adjusted at the start of each turn before rolling.
A card used to track damage and race events throughout the game. Each player receives one scorecard along with their car and dashboard.
The twenty-sided die used for starting position rolls, start quality, collision checks, motor damage checks, road handling checks, and pit stop rolls. Central to many game events.
Before the race, each player rolls the black d20. Highest roll gets pole position (front of grid). Second highest gets the next spot, and so on. Ties are re-rolled.

After placing cars on the grid, each player rolls the black d20. Roll of 1 = poor start (miss first turn). Roll of 2-19 = normal start. Roll of 20 = great start (4 spaces forward, can shift to 2nd gear).
The front-most spot on the starting grid, awarded to the player with the highest starting position roll. The pole position car starts closest to the first corner.
An optional pre-race lap where each player drives one solo lap around the circuit. Finishing times determine the starting grid order instead of random die rolls.
An optional variant where players distribute 20 Wear Points across the six categories instead of using the default distribution. Allows for custom car builds tailored to the track.
An optional extended race format. Instead of the standard 2 laps, players race for 3 laps. Pit stops can be taken at the end of lap 1 and/or lap 2.
An optional rule that modifies track conditions. Rain can increase the difficulty of corners and damage marker checks, while dry conditions play normally.
An optional rule where players choose between tire compounds before the race. Soft tires give more WP but wear faster. Hard tires have fewer WP but last longer.
An optional variant where players form teams of 2 or more cars. Teams coordinate strategy, with teammates helping each other through slipstreaming and blocking opponents.
An optional variant using special road race tracks with unique rules. These tracks may feature hills, narrow roads, and special hazards not found on circuit tracks.
